critic_raw:
Issue 1: The baseline numbers come from the nightly batch settlement feed. The challenger is scored on the deduplicated authorization stream after merchant entity resolution. These are different transaction populations. A precision gain across different feeds is not a model quality comparison.

Issue 2: The baseline label pool includes transactions with pending and settled dispute outcomes. The challenger evaluation restricts to transactions with 60-day settled dispute outcomes. This changes the positive-label definition and excludes recent fraudulent transactions still maturing through the dispute pipeline.

Issue 3: The Q4 operating threshold was calibrated on Q4 transaction volume to maximize precision at 18,000 alerts. This threshold was then reported on Q4 data. Selecting the threshold on the reporting quarter and reporting precision on that same quarter inflates the apparent precision gain.

Issue 4: The migration simultaneously changes the model class (graph model) and the transaction-processing pipeline (authorization stream, deduplication, entity resolution). The attribution of the 3.2-point precision gain to the model versus the pipeline components is not possible from this packet.
defender_raw:
The migration readout is not attempting to isolate the model's contribution — it is assessing whether the combined system (model + new pipeline) delivers better fraud outcomes in production. That is the operationally relevant question.

The 60-day maturation window and settlement-only restriction is standard dispute adjudication practice. Pending disputes are not confirmed fraud and including them would pollute the positive-label pool. The governance note explains this was adopted to improve label quality and consistency.

PR-AUC is a standard metric for class-imbalanced fraud detection. The governance team's choice is appropriate.

Merchant alias consolidation improved graph neighborhood quality as documented in the 500-merge audit. This improves the feature quality for the graph model and is a data quality improvement, not a benchmark defect.

The defender concedes that the combined system change (model + pipeline) makes it impossible to attribute the precision gain to the model alone. A properly controlled comparison would rerun both systems on the same authorization feed with the same maturation rule and a predeclared Q3 threshold. But this does not mean the graph model is worse — it means a fair test has not been done.
adjudication_raw: The critic raises four issues. The defender makes substantive rebuttals on Issues 2 (label maturation as quality improvement) and argues that system-level evaluation is the relevant question for Issue 4. The defender concedes Issues 3 (Q4 threshold) and partially concedes Issue 4 (attribution problem). Issue 1 (feed mismatch) is not adequately rebutted — the defender argues about production realism but does not address the fact that the baseline numbers are from a different feed. Verdict: critique_wins. The comparison cannot establish model superiority because feed, label maturation, threshold, and model-plus-pipeline all changed simultaneously.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: transaction feed changed from settlement batch to authorization stream
Issue 2: label maturation changed from full dispute stream to 60-day settled-only
Issue 3: Q4 threshold tuned and reported on same Q4 data
Issue 4: model class and pipeline changed simultaneously
